title: Moon potatoes can take root
html_body:
Today in Science and science: The shrinking future of fish, flies falling out of favor, and more
Brought to you by Eppendorf & Science Prize for Neurobiology

Now accepting entries for the US$ 25,000
Eppendorf & Science Prize for Neurobiology
Application Deadline June 15, 2026
www.eppendorf.com/prize
View this email in your browser
17 March 2026
Today’s Future News looks at whether model organisms are falling out of fashion. But first, catch up on the latest science news, including the potential for potatoes on the Moon and an ancient loom.
Climate Change  |  Science
The shrinking future of fish
Fish are an essential source of protein around the globe—and one that will likely be impacted by global warming. Scientists have long tried to predict how rising temperatures will affect fisheries, with most forecasts focusing on ecological changes, but the fish themselves can also evolve. Indeed, evolutionary adaptations to warming oceans may further reduce the fish that can be sustainably harvested worldwide.

For many fish species, there is a tradeoff between growth and reproduction: Larger fish tend to produce more young, but investing in growth and delaying reproduction can be risky, since individuals may die before spawning at all. Warmer temperatures intensify that tradeoff by increasing metabolism and mortality.

Using a life-history model linking temperature, growth, mortality, and reproduction, researchers simulated how fish populations might evolve in warming oceans. Their results suggest that in warmer waters, fish will mature earlier and at smaller sizes, an evolutionary shift that allows them to reproduce sooner but ultimately produce fewer offspring over their lifetimes.

Examining 43 commercially important fisheries, researchers estimated that warming waters’ ecological effects alone could reduce sustainable yields by about 14% under a moderate climate scenario. When evolutionary responses were included, projected declines rose to about 22%. Evidence from genetic studies suggests that many fish populations possess enough variation for such evolutionary shifts to occur. Smaller body sizes could also ripple through marine ecosystems, altering predator-prey relationships that depend on size.

Writing in a related Science Perspective, biologists Joseph Travis and David Reznick note that the model ultimately asks a broader question about the future of seafood supplies—predicting what people “might no longer have for dinner in 2090.”
Read the Science paper
Read the Perspective
AstroAgriculture  |  News from Science
Moon potatoes can take root
Harvested plants—small weeds called Arabidopsis—grown in lunar soil are placed in a vial for analysis.  Tyler Jones/UF/IFAS
In The Martian, astronaut Mark Watney survives by turning the barren soil of Mars into a potato farm, coaxing life out of dust in a bid to stay alive. Scientists on Earth would rather not wait for that kind of emergency. Researchers are already testing how to grow crops like potatoes on the Moon, trying to solve the problem of space farming before anyone runs out of food.

Of course, “turning an inorganic, inhospitable bucket of glorified sand into something that can support plant growth is complex,” noted Anna-Lisa Paul, a plant molecular biologist not involved with the work. Lunar regolith—the loose, powdery layer that blankets the Moon’s surface—contains no organic matter, which makes it a challenging environment for growing plants. In a new study posted as a bioRxiv preprint, researchers tested how well potatoes grow in several different regolith simulants , using them to approximate the lunar ground and study whether crops could survive and remain nutritious if future astronauts tried to farm in it.

The researchers mixed vermicompost—organic waste from worms—into five separate simulants, representing different types of lunar geology, and planted five potatoes in each kind. After almost two months of growth, the tubers were harvested, freeze-dried, and ground up for testing. Analysis of the crops showed that growth in lunar regolith activated stress-related genes, including those involved in photosynthesis and protein modification, and had higher concentrations of copper and zinc. The plants’ nutritional value, though, was similar to potatoes grown in Earthen soil—a surprise to the scientists, who expected lower levels of nutrition.
Read the full story
Archaeology  |  News from Science
Bronze Age loom weaves a tale of ancient textiles
When a fire ripped through a wealthy Iberian village 3450 years ago, it left behind heaps of rubble that trapped tools, pots, and jewelry—and, archaeologists argue in a new Antiquity study, one of the oldest looms ever found. The charred, meter-long pine beams, stone weights, and fiber cords open a window on an ancient economy, and perhaps on Bronze Age fashion.

Beginning around 2000 BCE, written sources from Mesopotamia, Mycenae, Egypt and elsewhere indicate that looms and weaving had become a part of daily life. But because they were made of perishable wood, finding an actual loom from prehistoric Europe is extremely rare. “What we find in archaeology is usually just the loom weights, because everything else decays ,” said archaeologist Karina Grömer, who wasn’t involved in the new study. “To find a loom like this, just as it was standing there 3500 years ago, is really remarkable.”

Archaeologists found the loom’s wooden frame when they excavated a site near modern-day Alicante, Spain, in 2008. Close by lay nearly 50 clay loom weights, along with tough grass fibers probably used to lash the frame together—key clues the structure was used for weaving, rather than being simply debris from the collapsed house, the new paper argues. The weights were unusually light, potentially suggesting the weavers were working with delicate material such as sheep’s wool, which would have been a relatively new material in the region.
Read the full story
SPONSORED
Eppendorf & Science Prize for Neurobiology: Call for Entries 2026
This prize is awarded to young scientists for their outstanding contributions to neurobiological research based on experimental methods of molecular, cellular, systems, or organismic biology. Researchers not older than 35 years are invited to apply.
Apply Now
Future News
A new analysis suggests these famous models are no longer in scientific fashion.  (TOP left) Chris Mattison/NPL/Minden; (TOP RIGHT) Hermann Eisenbeiss/Science Source; (BOTTOM) Steve Gschmeissner/Science Source
Whither worms and fruit flies?
In the early 1990s, Thomas Hunt Morgan popularized the fruit fly Drosophila melanogaster for genetics research, launching it into the select company of widely studied “model” organisms. But the share of fruit fly papers in the scientific literature over the past 30 years has dropped by more than 90%, according to a new analysis.

The research, posted in a preprint last week, suggests that interest in model organisms overall may be waning—it found a 68% decrease in the proportion of studies on eight of them, including fruit flies, the house mouse Mus musculus, the nematode Caenorhabditis elegans, the baker’s yeast Saccharomyces cerevisiae, and the thale cress Arabidopsis thaliana. The study tracked published papers over a 30-year period in 48 journals and found the decline in both the journals with the highest profiles, such as Science, Cell, and Nature, and lower impact publications.

The result “is pretty damning” about the state of model organism work, says Nicholas Tolwinski, a biologist who studies aging and Drosophila  and wasn’t involved with the preprint. Tolwinski worries that the decline could make top journals more reluctant to publish papers on these species, leading to funding reductions and “eventually the complete loss of [model organism] labs.” Nicolas Gompel, senior author on the preprint and a geneticist and evolutionary biologist, calls the apparent change “really bad.” He says it “looks like model organisms are losing their original identity as the systems to discover fundamental properties of life.”

Still, other researchers question aspects of the analysis, such as the choice of journals, and argue there’s nothing to fret about. From this data it’s “not necessary to conclude that model organism use is down,” says Rachel Ankeny, a philosopher who studies model organism research. Moreover, she says, a reduction might indicate that the research has been successful. “If model organism work has done its job, it should be falling away.”
Read the full story
Et Cetera
Moonlighting to make ends meet
In January, Nature polled scientists about the economics of getting a degree. Just under half of the 1200 respondents said they have or had a side hustle to afford their Ph.D. program. “The quality of life [provided by] my current stipend (same amount as for 10 years ago) definitely has not been maintained with the rising cost of living,“ wrote one respondent. “Within a few years I think most people would struggle and need to begin a side hustle or second job.”
Read more at Nature
Placing the past people of Palau
Archaeologically speaking, Palau bucks the Pacific Island trend, which has left researchers wondering: Who were the first Palauans, and where did they come from? Now, genetic evidence confirms Palau’s first settlers descended from Southeast Asians who had intermingled with the Papuans, Indigenous peoples who settled the island of New Guinea some 50,000 years ago. “It’s lovely to see a piece of Pacific history—which we’ve traditionally been at a bit of a loss to explain—finally start to come together into a more understandable story,” said one computational biologist.
Cell Paper  |  Read more at News from Science
Shining a light on stinky Styrofoam
Less than 1% of the 20,000 tons of polystyrene produced each year is recycled. In part, that’s because the methods used to break down this plastic, often called Styrofoam, are energy intensive. But researchers have found a way to combine polystyrene waste with excess sulfur to produce valuable products—a process that only requires sunlight. “We address both plastic pollution and the efficient utilization of sulfur resources,” explained one of the researchers.
Journal of the American Chemical Society Paper  |  Read more at Chemistry World
Humanity has failed to eliminate poverty and instead of addressing socioeconomic causes of food insecurity, agricultural research and practice are called upon to compensate. This is not only unfair but bound to fail.
EDITORIAL  |  12 March 2026  |  Christoph Müller
Last but not least
I am somewhat disappointed with myself that I lived in Florida for six years and yet never made it out to Dania Beach to see a wild monkey. At least I tried to spot the wallabies on O‘ahu when I lived in Hawai‘i!
Christie Wilcox, Editor, ScienceAdviser

With contributions from Ana Georgescu, Perri Thaler, Phie Jacobs, and Mitch Leslie

already testing how to grow crops like potatoes on the Moon
one of the oldest looms ever found
the share of fruit fly papers in the scientific literature over the past 30 years has dropped by more than 90%
Dania Beach to see a wild monkey
